lede: Astrology · โหราศาสตร์ accounts for 388 catalogued manuscripts, 388 of them flagged research-priority. It clusters in Phayao (23% of the corpus for this genre), ahead of Lampang and Chiang Mai. Nearly all (97%) are written in Tham Lanna · อักษรธรรมล้านนา script. By support it leans to mulberry paper (saa) · กระดาษสา (76%) over palm-leaf · ใบลาน (22%). Dated witnesses run 1578–1973 CE (38 of 388 carry a date).

authored.title: Astrology
authored.body_html:
What this is

Horā (Lanna holasat / horasat) is the reckoning of time and fortune: the lunar-solar calendar (patithin), the choice of auspicious and inauspicious days (wan di wan sia), the casting of a birth-chart (duang chata), and the long planetary periods that a diviner reads over a life. It descends from Indian jyotiṣa, carried north through Mon and Burmese intermediaries and then reworked in Tai hands, so a Lanna horā manuscript sits at the meeting point of Sanskritic astronomy and local almanac practice.

For this archive this is a research-priority genre. Horā is living knowledge — a mo duu still consults these tables today — which makes the manuscripts easy to contextualise against active practice and a natural bridge between the historical record and the contemporary vernacular.

The shape of the collection

Horā in this catalogue is an upper-northern tradition. It clusters in Phayao and Lampang ahead of Chiang Mai, Nan and Chiang Rai — the old muang of the Ping, Wang and upper Yom basins — with the largest single holdings at Wat Chiang Man, Wat Phra That Lampang Luang and Wat Si Khom Kham. Almost every witness is written in Tham Lanna script in the mixed Pali-and-Lan Na register typical of the region's learned texts.

The most telling pattern is physical. Where the canonical genres are overwhelmingly palm-leaf, horā leans the other way — toward mulberry-paper folding books (phap sa). That is not an accident of survival: an almanac is a working reference, opened flat and read across a spread, and the concertina phap sa is the tool for the job in a way a bound stack of palm-leaves is not. When you browse this genre and see paper outrunning leaf, you are seeing the genre's use fossilised in its support.

A note on the horā / saiyasat boundary

Many titles here read Holasat plainly, but a good number pair it with Sainyasat (saiyasat, protective magic) or katha in a single volume — "Katha tang tang, Sainyasat", "Holasat, Tholanisan". In practice the diviner and the ritualist were often the same person, and a household manual bound the calendar next to the protective verse. The catalogue's genre line records the dominant content, but the real object is frequently a hybrid. The companion article on Magic & Ritual is the other half of this world.

Notes

  • Read alongside the raw label. A handful of rows were reclaimed into this genre from Undetermined / Miscellany by title markers (holasat, horasat, phrommachat); their catalogue metadata is thin and worth checking against the image before citing.
  • Dates are sparse. Only a minority of witnesses carry a date; the span shown in the facts panel is anchored by those few and should not be read as the genre's true range.
  • The Phrommachat (illustrated almanac of the twelve-year animal cycle) is the most recognisable horā sub-type and a good candidate for its own article once the image download phase fills in the plates.

What the bots have noticed

Auto-derived from the catalogue and the curiosity bots — refreshed automatically, not hand-written. Each note links to its finding.

  • Where the stars were written down — 2 provinces whose surviving corpus leans toward astrology — the reading of time, fate, and the calendar — well above the corpus norm. see the finding →
